lines.greek: υἱός, ἀτὰρ Ταφίοισι φιληρέτμοισιν ἀνάσσω.
νῦν δʼ ὧδε ξὺν νηὶ κατήλυθον ἠδʼ ἑτάροισιν
πλέων ἐπὶ οἴνοπα πόντον ἐπʼ ἀλλοθρόους ἀνθρώπους,
ἐς Τεμέσην μετὰ χαλκόν, ἄγω δʼ αἴθωνα σίδηρον.
νηῦς δέ μοι ἥδʼ ἕστηκεν ἐπʼ ἀγροῦ νόσφι πόληος,
ἐν λιμένι Ῥείθρῳ ὑπὸ Νηίῳ ὑλήεντι.
ξεῖνοι δʼ ἀλλήλων πατρώιοι εὐχόμεθʼ εἶναι
ἐξ ἀρχῆς, εἴ πέρ τε γέροντʼ εἴρηαι ἐπελθὼν
Λαέρτην ἥρωα, τὸν οὐκέτι φασὶ πόλινδε
ἔρχεσθʼ, ἀλλʼ ἀπάνευθεν ἐπʼ ἀγροῦ πήματα πάσχειν
γρηὶ σὺν ἀμφιπόλῳ, ἥ οἱ βρῶσίν τε πόσιν τε
παρτιθεῖ, εὖτʼ ἄν μιν κάματος κατὰ γυῖα λάβῃσιν
ἑρπύζοντʼ ἀνὰ γουνὸν ἀλωῆς οἰνοπέδοιο.
νῦν δʼ ἦλθον· δὴ γάρ μιν ἔφαντʼ ἐπιδήμιον εἶναι,
σὸν πατέρʼ· ἀλλά νυ τόν γε θεοὶ βλάπτουσι κελεύθου.
οὐ γάρ πω τέθνηκεν ἐπὶ χθονὶ δῖος Ὀδυσσεύς,
ἀλλʼ ἔτι που ζωὸς κατερύκεται εὐρέι πόντῳ
νήσῳ ἐν ἀμφιρύτῃ, χαλεποὶ δέ μιν ἄνδρες ἔχουσιν
ἄγριοι, οἵ που κεῖνον ἐρυκανόωσʼ ἀέκοντα.
αὐτὰρ νῦν τοι ἐγὼ μαντεύσομαι, ὡς ἐνὶ θυμῷ

interlinear: the-son; and over-the-Taphians, oar-loving, I-rule.
and-now thus with a-ship I-have-come-down, and with-comrades,
sailing over the-wine-dark sea to men of-strange-speech,
to Temese after copper, and-I-bring shining iron.
and-my ship, this-one, stands by-the-field, apart-from the-city,
in the-harbour Rheithron, beneath Neion the-wooded.
and-guest-friends of-one-another, from-our-fathers, we-declare to-be
from the-beginning, if indeed the-old-man you-should-ask, going-to-him,
Laertes the-hero, whom no-longer, they-say, to-the-city
does-he-come, but far-off in-the-field woes he-suffers
with-an-aged-woman as-handmaid, who for-him food and drink
sets-beside-him, whenever him weariness through his-limbs takes
as-he-creeps along the-slope of-his-orchard, his-vineyard-plot.
and-now I-came; for indeed him they-said at-home to-be,
your father; but now him the-gods are-thwarting of-his-road.
for not yet has-he-died upon the-earth, goodly Odysseus,
but still somewhere alive he-is-held-back on the-broad sea,
on-an-island sea-girt; and-harsh men him hold,
savage ones, who somewhere that-man keep-back, unwilling.
but now to-you I will-prophesy, as in my-heart
literal: his son, and I rule over the oar-loving Taphians.
And now I have put in here with my ship and my comrades,
sailing over the wine-dark sea to men of strange speech,
to Temese in quest of copper, and I bring shining iron.
My ship lies yonder beside the field, away from the city,
in the harbour of Rheithron, beneath wooded Neion.
We declare that we are guest-friends of one another, from our fathers,
from of old, if you should go and ask the old man,
Laertes the hero, who, they say, comes no longer
to the city, but far off in the fields suffers woes
with an aged woman as his handmaid, who sets food and drink
beside him, whenever weariness takes hold of his limbs
as he creeps along the slope of his vineyard plot.
And now I have come, for indeed they said that he was at home,
your father; but now the gods are thwarting him of his road.
For goodly Odysseus has not yet died upon the earth,
but somewhere he is still alive and held back on the broad sea,
on a sea-girt island; and harsh men hold him,
savage men, who keep him there against his will.
But now I will prophesy to you, as in my heart
translations.elizabethan:
I am his son, and over Taphian men,
lovers of oars, I hold the lordship. Here
I put in now with ship and crew, and sail
across the wine-dark water to a people
of alien speech, to Temese, for bronze,
and shining iron do I carry hence.
My ship lies yonder by the fields, aloof
from any town, in Rheithron's harbourage
beneath the wooded slope of Neion.
Guest-friends we are, thy house and mine, from old,
our fathers' friendship. Go and ask the man,
old Laertes, that hero, who they say
comes to the city now no more, but bears
his sorrows in the country far away,
one aged woman only to his hand,
who sets him food and drink when weariness
has fallen on his limbs, as up the slope
of his own vineyard plot he slowly creeps.
I came because they told me he was home,
thy father. But the gods obstruct his road.
For he is not yet dead upon the earth,
that goodly man, Odysseus. Somewhere still
he lives, kept back upon the open sea,
upon an island girdled all with waves,
and hard men hold him, savage, and against
his will they keep him there. But hear me now:
I shall foretell thee what the gods have set
within my heart, and how I think it falls.
translations.modernist:
I am his son. I rule the Taphians. They love the oar. I came in here with my ship and my crew, sailing the wine-dark sea to men who speak another language. I am going to Temese for copper. I carry shining iron.

My ship is out there by the fields, away from the town, in the harbor of Rheithron under the woods of Neion.

Your house and mine are guest-friends, and were in our fathers' time. Ask the old man if you like. Laertes. They say he does not come into the city any more. He is out in the country with his troubles. One old woman looks after him. She puts food and drink in front of him when the tiredness gets into his legs, after he has crawled up the slope of his vineyard.

I came because they told me your father was home. The gods are keeping him off the road. He is not dead. Odysseus is not lying dead anywhere on this earth. He is alive and they are holding him out on the wide sea, on an island with water all around it. Hard men have him. They are savages, and they keep him there, and he does not want to stay.

Now I will tell you what is coming. The gods have put it in my heart.
translations.storybook:
I am his son, and I am the chief of the Taphian people, who love their oars. I have come here in my own ship with my own crew, sailing across the wine-dark sea to visit people who speak a different language. I am on my way to Temese to trade for copper, and I have a cargo of shining iron with me.

My ship is anchored out there past the fields, well away from the town, in the harbour of Rheithron, underneath the woods of Neion.

And you should know that your family and mine have been friends for a very long time, ever since our fathers' days! If you do not believe me, you can go and ask old Laertes. They tell me he does not come into the city any more. He lives far out in the country now, very sad, with only one old woman to look after him. She brings him his food and his drink when his poor legs get tired, after he has crawled all the way up the hill of his vineyard.

I came because somebody told me your father was home again. But the gods are blocking his road. Now listen: your father is not dead. Odysseus is not lying dead anywhere in the world! He is alive, out on the wide sea, on an island with water all around it, and rough, savage men are keeping him there when he wants nothing more than to leave.

And now I am going to tell you what is going to happen, for the gods have put it into my heart.

murray.text: My ship lies yonder beside the fields away from the city, in the harbor of Rheithron, under woody Neion. Friends of one another do we declare ourselves to be, even as our fathers were, friends from of old. Nay, if thou wilt, go and ask the old warrior Laertes, who, they say,

comes no more to the city, but afar in the fields suffers woes attended by an aged woman as his handmaid, who sets before him food and drink, after weariness has laid hold of his limbs, as he creeps along the slope of his vineyard plot. And now am I come, for of a truth men said that he,

thy father, was among his people; but lo, the gods are thwarting him of his return. For not yet has goodly Odysseus perished on the earth, but still, I ween, he lives and is held back on the broad sea in a sea-girt isle, and cruel men keep him, a savage folk, that constrain him, haply sore against his will.

Nay, I will now prophesy to thee, as the immortals put it in my heart, and as I think it shall be brought to pass, though I am in no wise a soothsayer, nor one versed in the signs of birds. Not much longer shall he be absent from his dear native land, no, not though bonds of iron hold him.
